title: Ontology SDK - Python OSDK - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Python OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Python SDK for accessing Ontology objects, performing filtering, aggregations, and executing actions programmatically from Python environments.

extractedEntities.name: OSDK
extractedEntities.description: Ontology Software Development Kit providing type-safe, code-generated client libraries for interacting with the Ontology data model across multiple languages.

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for generating Ontology-specific SDK documentation and managing developer configurations.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity representing a real-world concept (e.g., Restaurant) with typed properties, a primary key, and queryable through SDK clients.

extractedEntities.name: Property
extractedEntities.description: Typed attribute of an Object Type (e.g., restaurantId: String, numberOfReviews: Integer) exposed through the SDK as fields on returned objects.

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: A designated Property that uniquely identifies each object instance within an Object Type, used for single-object lookups.

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: A queryable collection of objects of a given Object Type, supporting filtering, ordering, pagination, and aggregation operations.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept representing executable operations that can modify objects, invoked through SDK apply methods with typed parameters.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Backend storage technology for Object Types that supports unlimited result sets, unlike the legacy Phonograph backend which limits to 10,000 results.

extractedEntities.name: Phonograph
extractedEntities.description: Legacy object storage backend with a 10,000 result limit per query, superseded by Object Storage V2.

extractedEntities.name: Page Token Pagination
extractedEntities.description: SDK pagination mechanism using nextPageToken for iterating through large result sets with configurable page sizes up to 10,000.

extractedEntities.name: Code-Generated SDK
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where SDK client code is auto-generated from Ontology schema definitions, providing type-safe access to Object Types, Properties, and Actions.

extractedEntities.name: Aggregation API
extractedEntities.description: SDK capability to compute aggregate values (count, min, max, sum, avg, approximateDistinct) over ObjectSets with optional groupBy clauses.

keyInsights: Python OSDK provides programmatic access to the full Ontology data model including objects, filtering, aggregations, and action execution.
Object Storage V2 removes the 10,000 result limit that existed with the legacy Phonograph backend, enabling unlimited queries.
SDK documentation can be auto-generated specific to a customer's Ontology via Developer Console, providing tailored code examples.
Pagination uses a page token pattern with configurable page sizes up to 10,000 objects per page.
The SDK exposes aggregation operations (count, min, max, sum, avg, approximateDistinct) with groupBy support over ObjectSets.
Object responses include a __rid (resource identifier) and __primaryKey alongside all typed properties.

title: Data Lineage - Graphs - Explore artifacts and ontology entities - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for visualizing and exploring the dependency graph between datasets, artifacts, and Ontology entities, showing how data flows through the platform.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity that can be traced back to its source datasets through Data Lineage, showing which datasets back which Object Types.

extractedEntities.name: Link Type
extractedEntities.description: Relationship definition between Object Types, visualizable within the Data Lineage graph to understand connections between datasets and Ontology structures.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for configuring Object Types, accessible directly from Data Lineage when viewing an Object Type's configuration.

extractedEntities.name: Contour
extractedEntities.description: Foundry visualization tool whose outputs appear as related artifacts in Data Lineage graphs.

extractedEntities.name: Slate
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application builder whose applications appear as related artifacts in Data Lineage graphs.

extractedEntities.name: Data Provenance Tracking
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of tracing Ontology entities back through their lineage to source datasets, enabling understanding of how business objects are derived from raw data.

extractedEntities.name: Related Artifacts Panel
extractedEntities.description: Data Lineage UI component that shows Foundry resources (visualizations, applications) related to a selected dataset.

keyInsights: Data Lineage bridges the gap between raw datasets and Ontology entities, showing which datasets back which Object Types.
Object Types can be discovered from datasets via the View Node Properties panel, and their configuration accessed directly in Ontology Manager.
Link Types between Object Types can be visualized within the lineage graph, showing how Ontology relationships connect to underlying data.
Data Lineage serves as a cross-cutting navigation tool connecting datasets to Contour visualizations, Slate applications, and Ontology entities.
Users can search for and add Object Types to lineage graphs to understand connections between datasets and business-level Ontology constructs.

title: Code Repositories - Transforms - Create Transforms - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Code Repositories
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for authoring data transformation logic using Python, Java, or SQL, with a wizard-driven file template system for bootstrapping transforms.

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Core Foundry data processing unit that takes one or more input datasets/models and produces a single output, encapsulating computational logic and resource declarations.

extractedEntities.name: Lightweight Transform
extractedEntities.description: Single-node transform type recommended for datasets under 10 million rows, running on a single compute node without distributed processing overhead.

extractedEntities.name: Spark Transform
extractedEntities.description: Distributed transform type leveraging Apache Spark for datasets exceeding 10 million rows, providing parallel computation across multiple nodes.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data integration and analytics platform providing the execution environment for transforms, pipelines, and Ontology management.

extractedEntities.name: Transform Template Wizard
extractedEntities.description: Code Repositories UI feature that bootstraps transform files from templates with live preview, input/output configuration, and automatic validation.

keyInsights: Transforms are the fundamental building blocks of Foundry data processing, consuming input datasets and producing output datasets.
Two transform types exist: Lightweight (single-node, <10M rows) and Spark (distributed, >10M rows), chosen based on data scale.
Code Repositories provides a template wizard with live preview and automatic validation for bootstrapping transforms.
Input and output resources must reside within the same Foundry project as the repository, enforcing project-level isolation.
Templates automatically configure required dependencies and backing libraries when generating transform files.

title: Python - Basic transforms - Transforms - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Transform
extractedEntities.description: Core Foundry data processing building block consisting of decorators (inputs/outputs/config), a function signature, and compute logic that defines how data flows from inputs to outputs.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Transforms
extractedEntities.description: The foundry_transforms Python library providing the @transform decorator, Input/Output classes, and compute engine abstractions for defining data pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: Transform Decorator
extractedEntities.description: Python decorator (@transform.using) that declares inputs and outputs for a transform function, connecting it to Foundry's orchestration layer.

extractedEntities.name: Polars Compute Engine
extractedEntities.description: Single-node DataFrame engine known for performance and stability, available as an alternative to pandas for lightweight transforms.

extractedEntities.name: Pandas Compute Engine
extractedEntities.description: Widely adopted single-node DataFrame engine available for lightweight transforms in Foundry.

extractedEntities.name: DuckDB Compute Engine
extractedEntities.description: SQL-based compute engine available within Foundry transforms, enabling SQL queries directly on input datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Spark Compute Engine
extractedEntities.description: Distributed multi-node compute engine for large-scale data processing in Foundry transforms.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool that registers transforms into Pipelines, providing a visual interface for pipeline composition.

keyInsights: Every Foundry transform has three components: decorators (declare I/O), function signature (matches declared I/O), and compute logic (actual processing).
Four compute engines are available: Polars, pandas, and DuckDB for single-node (lightweight) transforms, and Spark for distributed multi-node transforms.
Transforms default to single-node execution; Spark must be explicitly chosen for distributed processing of large datasets.
Transforms support multiple inputs and multiple outputs, enabling complex pipeline topologies beyond simple one-to-one mappings.
Polars is recommended over pandas for lightweight transforms due to superior performance and stability.

title: Data Connection - External Transforms - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: External Transform
extractedEntities.description: Transform variant that enables connections to external systems from Python transforms repositories, used for batch sync, export, and media sync workflows when standard Data Connection sources are insufficient.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry service managing connections to external systems, providing source types, authentication, and egress policies for data integration.

extractedEntities.name: Egress Policy
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection security mechanism controlling which external domains a transform can access, requiring explicit allowlisting of target URLs.

extractedEntities.name: External Systems Sidebar
extractedEntities.description: Code Repositories UI panel for creating and configuring Data Connection sources directly from within a transforms repository.

extractedEntities.name: Source Credentials
extractedEntities.description: Secure credential management system within Data Connection that stores and injects authentication tokens/secrets into external transform execution contexts.

extractedEntities.name: Virtual Table
extractedEntities.description: Pattern for accessing data from external systems that remain outside Foundry, where transforms reach out to the external system at query time.

extractedEntities.name: Code Repositories
extractedEntities.description: Foundry development environment for writing Python transforms that can import and interact with Data Connection sources.

keyInsights: External transforms extend standard Foundry transforms by allowing outbound connections to external REST APIs, databases, and other systems.
Data Connection sources can be directly imported into Code Repositories, superseding the older credentials-based legacy approach.
Egress policies provide security boundaries controlling which external domains a transform can reach, requiring explicit allowlisting.
External transforms support three primary workflows: batch sync (ingest), export (push out), and media sync (file transfer).
Virtual tables are a special case of external transforms where data remains in the external system and is queried on demand rather than copied into Foundry.
Authentication is managed through Data Connection source credentials, keeping secrets out of transform code.

title: Code Examples - Incremental Transforms - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Transform
extractedEntities.description: Transform pattern that processes only new or changed data since the last pipeline run, avoiding full recomputation and enabling efficient processing of growing datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Decorator
extractedEntities.description: Python decorator (@incremental) that wraps transform compute functions to enable incremental processing semantics, tracking which data has been previously processed.

extractedEntities.name: IncrementalTransformInput
extractedEntities.description: Specialized input type in incremental transforms providing access to only the new/changed data since the last run, with methods for reading current and previous state.

extractedEntities.name: IncrementalTransformOutput
extractedEntities.description: Specialized output type supporting set_mode('modify') for appending incremental results rather than overwriting the entire output dataset.

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type representing a full recomputation where all data is replaced, contrasting with incremental APPEND transactions.

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type where only new data is added to an existing dataset, enabling incremental processing semantics.

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Aggregation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern for computing aggregations incrementally by maintaining running totals and updating only with new data, avoiding full dataset scans.

keyInsights: Incremental transforms use the @incremental decorator to process only new data, checking ctx.is_incremental to branch between full and incremental logic.
Output.set_mode('modify') enables appending incremental results rather than overwriting, preserving previously computed data.
Incremental transforms must handle SNAPSHOT transactions gracefully, falling back to full recomputation when inputs are fully replaced.
Incremental aggregation patterns maintain running state and update only with new records, enabling efficient aggregation over growing datasets.
External API data can be fetched incrementally by tracking last-processed dates and page tokens across runs.

title: Introduction - API Reference - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Foundry API
extractedEntities.description: RESTful HTTP API for programmatic interaction with the Foundry platform, using OAuth 2.0 authentication and JSON request/response payloads.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform providing data integration, analytics, Ontology management, AI services, workflow services, and software delivery capabilities.

extractedEntities.name: OAuth 2.0
extractedEntities.description: Authentication protocol used by the Foundry API, requiring OAuth tokens for all API requests.

extractedEntities.name: Platform SDKs
extractedEntities.description: Language-specific client libraries that simplify interaction with the Foundry API, generated from the API specification for supported programming languages.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry API V2
extractedEntities.description: Version 2.0 of the Foundry REST API, the current recommended version for building applications on top of Palantir products.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology-Centric Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's core architectural pattern where the Ontology serves as the model of data, AI, and workflows, connecting Data Services, AI Services, Workflow Services, Security, and Software Delivery.

keyInsights: The Foundry API is a REST API using OAuth 2.0 for authentication, with JSON as the standard request/response format.
The Ontology sits at the center of Foundry's architecture, serving as the unified model connecting Data Services, AI Services, Workflow Services, Security, and Software Delivery.
Platform SDKs are available for supported languages to simplify API interactions, abstracting away raw HTTP calls.
API V2 is the current recommended version, with V1 still available for backward compatibility.
Data Connection exports are recommended over direct API calls for bulk data export use cases.

title: Available Connectors - REST APIs - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: REST API Source
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection source type enabling interactive HTTP requests to external REST endpoints from Foundry applications via Actions and Webhooks.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry service managing connections to external systems, providing source types for syncs, exports, and interactive API calls.

extractedEntities.name: Webhook Integration
extractedEntities.description: Pattern for connecting Foundry applications (e.g., Workshop buttons) to external REST endpoints via Action-triggered HTTP calls through the REST API source.

extractedEntities.name: REST API Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Authentication options for REST API sources including None, Basic, Bearer Token, and API Key, configured per-domain in Data Connection.

extractedEntities.name: External Transform REST Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of using external transforms in Code Repositories to implement custom sync and export logic against REST APIs, replacing legacy magritte-rest-v2 source type.

extractedEntities.name: Agent Egress Policy
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism allowing external transforms to access REST API sources inaccessible over the public internet by routing through a Foundry worker agent.

extractedEntities.name: OAuth Client Credentials Grant
extractedEntities.description: OAuth flow used by external transforms for server-to-server authentication with REST APIs, using client_id and certificate credentials.

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application builder whose buttons can trigger Webhook calls to REST API sources, enabling user-initiated external system interactions.

keyInsights: REST API sources in Data Connection are specifically for interactive HTTP requests via Webhooks/Actions, not for batch syncs or exports.
Syncs and exports to REST APIs should use external transforms in Code Repositories, not the REST API source type directly.
The legacy magritte-rest-v2 source type is deprecated in favor of the new REST API source for Webhooks and external transforms for syncs/exports.
Authentication options include None, Basic, Bearer Token, and API Key, with API Keys passable as query params or headers.
Workshop applications can trigger REST API calls via button-driven Webhook Actions, connecting UIs to external system workflows.
Agent-based workers enable external transforms to reach REST APIs on private networks not accessible over the public internet.

title: The Technical Anatomy of Palantir's Foundry Platform: An In-Depth Analysis - Medium

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's modular data platform built on a service mesh of hundreds of distinct microservices, providing data integration, analytics, Ontology management, and operational workflows.

extractedEntities.name: Service Mesh Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's modular architecture consisting of hundreds of distinct microservices operating in highly-available, redundant configurations with zero-downtime upgrades.

extractedEntities.name: Rubix Engine
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's auto-scaling engine that manages scaling across core services and the compute mesh using a containerization paradigm.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Semantic Model
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's structured semantic model that bridges raw data and end users by weaving data and models into a comprehensive business representation.

extractedEntities.name: Zero-Downtime Upgrade
extractedEntities.description: Foundry deployment pattern enabled by granular monitoring strategies that inform upgrade deployment and rollback procedures without service interruption.

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Storage Architecture
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's approach of deploying various storage technologies at different architectural tiers and selecting appropriate runtimes for different workloads.

extractedEntities.name: Contour
extractedEntities.description: Foundry analytics and visualization tool for exploring and analyzing data.

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool for time-series analysis and dashboarding.

keyInsights: Foundry's architecture is a service mesh of hundreds of microservices, each running in highly-available, redundant configurations.
The Rubix engine provides auto-scaling across both core Foundry services and the compute mesh using containerization.
Foundry is storage-agnostic, deploying different storage technologies at different architectural tiers rather than committing to a single paradigm.
The Ontology serves as a structured semantic model bridging raw data and business users, enabling the capture and integration of data back into the semantic foundation.
Security and data lineage are enforced consistently across every tier of the platform architecture.
Foundry supports Python, SQL, Java, TypeScript, JavaScript, and R across different paradigms (transforms, ML workflows, frontend applications).

title: Building Pipelines - Streaming Pipelines - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Continuously-running Foundry pipeline that processes data as it arrives with very low latency, making data accessible in the Ontology in under 15 seconds on average.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity that can be updated in near real-time via streaming pipelines, making streaming data immediately accessible for analysis.

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Foundry time-series analysis application that can display streaming data accessible through the Ontology.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Rules
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for defining and executing rules against streaming data, enabling automated decision-making on real-time information.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry visual tool for creating and managing streaming transforms within streaming pipelines.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Row Size Limit
extractedEntities.description: Technical constraint of 1MB maximum per individual row in streaming pipelines to ensure low-latency data transfers.

extractedEntities.name: Stateful Stream Processing
extractedEntities.description: Streaming pattern using windows or aggregations that maintain state, requiring careful design when changing stream logic to avoid state corruption.

keyInsights: Streaming pipelines enable sub-15-second data availability in the Ontology, making real-time operational decisions possible.
Streaming compute runs continuously, resulting in higher compute costs compared to batch or incremental pipelines.
A 1MB per-row size constraint exists for streaming data to maintain low-latency transfer guarantees.
Stateful operations (windows, aggregations) require careful design consideration when modifying stream logic to prevent state corruption.
Streaming data flows through to the Ontology and becomes immediately available in analysis tools like Quiver and Foundry Rules.

title: Health Checks - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Data Health
extractedEntities.description: Foundry service for monitoring and alerting on common issues across datasets, schedules, and tables using customizable health checks.

extractedEntities.name: Data Lineage
extractedEntities.description: Foundry tool that integrates with Data Health to color datasets by their health check status and display health information in the lineage graph.

extractedEntities.name: Health Check
extractedEntities.description: Monitoring rule applied to Foundry resources (datasets, schedules, tables) that detects issues related to status, time, size, content, and schema.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Preview
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for viewing dataset content that includes a Health tab for managing health checks on individual datasets and tables.

extractedEntities.name: Monitoring View
extractedEntities.description: Scalable monitoring approach for covering many similar resources automatically, complementing individual health checks when coverage needs to scale.

extractedEntities.name: Health Check Alerting
extractedEntities.description: Notification system supporting Foundry Notifications, emails, PagerDuty, Slack, and arbitrary REST webhooks for health check alerts.

keyInsights: Data Health monitors five categories of issues: dataset status, time, size, content, and schema, using customizable check rules.
Health checks can be created for datasets, schedules, and tables, and can also be defined programmatically through code.
Alerting integrates with Foundry Notifications, email, PagerDuty, Slack, and arbitrary REST webhooks for flexible notification delivery.
Data Lineage visualizes health check status by coloring datasets in the lineage graph, providing at-a-glance pipeline health overview.
Monitoring Views provide a scalable alternative to individual health checks when many similar resources need consistent monitoring coverage.

title: Building Pipelines - Types of Pipelines - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Batch Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type that fully recomputes every dataset on each run, offering low complexity but high latency and compute cost, with low resilience to data scale changes.

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline type that processes only new/changed data since the last run, offering low latency and compute cost with medium complexity and high resilience to data scale.

extractedEntities.name: Streaming Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Continuously-running pipeline that processes data as it arrives, offering very low latency but high complexity and compute cost with high resilience to data scale.

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform providing three pipeline types with different latency, complexity, cost, and scale resilience tradeoffs.

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type used by batch pipelines where all data is fully replaced on each run.

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Dataset transaction type used by incremental pipelines where only new data is added to existing datasets.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Progression Pattern
extractedEntities.description: Recommended approach of starting with batch pipelines for validation, then extending to incremental as scale demands, keeping streaming for real-time requirements.

keyInsights: Three pipeline types exist with clear tradeoff dimensions: batch (simple/expensive), incremental (moderate/efficient), streaming (complex/real-time).
Batch pipelines should be the starting point for pipeline development, with incremental extension as data scale validates the use case.
Incremental pipelines offer the best balance of latency (minutes) and compute cost for growing datasets, but require understanding of transactions.
Streaming pipelines provide the lowest latency (seconds) but carry the highest complexity and continuous compute costs.
Python or Java are recommended for batch pipelines that may need future incremental support, as SQL does not support incremental computation.
Foundry also offers 'faster' variants of batch and incremental pipelines that sacrifice some expression support for improved performance.

title: Building Pipelines - Incremental Pipelines - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Pipeline architecture that processes only new or changed data since the last run, enabling minutes-level latency while minimizing compute costs compared to full recomputation.

extractedEntities.name: Dataset Transaction Model
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's data versioning system using APPEND and SNAPSHOT transactions to track how datasets change over time, fundamental to incremental processing.

extractedEntities.name: APPEND Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Transaction type that adds new data to an existing dataset without modifying previous data, enabling incremental pipelines to process only the delta.

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Transaction
extractedEntities.description: Transaction type that fully replaces dataset contents, requiring incremental pipelines to gracefully fall back to full recomputation.

extractedEntities.name: Spark File Partitioning
extractedEntities.description: Technical concern for long-running incremental pipelines where many APPEND transactions create numerous small files, requiring understanding of Spark's file handling for performance.

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry visual tool that supports creating incremental pipeline configurations.

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry service that provides incremental sync capabilities for bringing data into Foundry incrementally.

keyInsights: Incremental pipelines require deep understanding of Foundry's transaction model (APPEND vs SNAPSHOT) to implement correctly.
Transform logic must be resilient to unexpected SNAPSHOT transactions on inputs, gracefully falling back to full recomputation.
Long-running incremental pipelines accumulate many small files from APPEND transactions, requiring Spark partitioning management for sustained performance.
Incremental syncs in Data Connection are the first step in building an end-to-end incremental pipeline.
Unit testing is strongly recommended before production deployment to validate behavior under both incremental and snapshot scenarios.

title: A Practical Guide to Using the @incremental Decorator in Foundry - Medium

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Decorator
extractedEntities.description: Python decorator (@incremental()) that wraps a transform's compute function to enable incremental computation, providing IncrementalTransformInput and IncrementalTransformOutput types.

extractedEntities.name: Incremental Transform
extractedEntities.description: Transform pattern that processes only changed data by detecting APPEND transactions and applying delta logic, with fallback to full recomputation on SNAPSHOT transactions.

extractedEntities.name: Transform Decorator
extractedEntities.description: Core Python decorator (@transform) that defines inputs and outputs for a computation, connecting transform logic to Foundry's orchestration layer.

extractedEntities.name: IncrementalTransformInput
extractedEntities.description: Specialized input type provided by the @incremental decorator that gives access to only the changed data since the last build run.

extractedEntities.name: IncrementalTransformOutput
extractedEntities.description: Specialized output type provided by the @incremental decorator supporting modify mode for appending results rather than full replacement.

extractedEntities.name: SNAPSHOT Build
extractedEntities.description: Build mode where the entire dataset is processed from scratch, contrasting with incremental builds that only process new data.

extractedEntities.name: Financial Transaction Processing
extractedEntities.description: Use case where incremental transforms process daily new transactions from a large historical dataset, avoiding full recomputation of millions of rows.

keyInsights: The @incremental decorator changes input/output types to IncrementalTransformInput/IncrementalTransformOutput, providing delta-only data access.
Incremental transforms must handle both incremental (APPEND) and snapshot (full recompute) scenarios in the same code path.
The ctx.is_incremental flag allows branching logic between incremental and snapshot processing within a single transform function.
Financial transaction processing is a canonical use case where incremental transforms avoid reprocessing millions of historical rows daily.
The transform decorator remains the foundation; @incremental wraps it to add incremental semantics without changing the core transform pattern.

title: Indexing - Funnel Batch Pipelines - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Funnel Batch Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Internal Foundry job pipeline that orchestrates efficient indexing of data from datasources and user edits into Object Storage V2 through a four-stage process.

extractedEntities.name: Changelog Stage
extractedEntities.description: First stage of Funnel batch pipeline that computes data differences for all datasources when new data arrives, creating intermediate changelog datasets with APPEND transactions.

extractedEntities.name: Merge Changes Stage
extractedEntities.description: Second stage that joins changelog datasets and user edits by primary key to merge all changes into a unified dataset.

extractedEntities.name: Indexing Stage
extractedEntities.description: Third stage that converts merged change rows into format compatible with configured object databases (e.g., OSv2 index files).

extractedEntities.name: Hydration Stage
extractedEntities.description: Final stage where object databases prepare indexed data for querying by downloading index files to search node disks, enabling the Object Type to be queried.

extractedEntities.name: Object Storage V2
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's canonical object database that stores indexed Object Type data, receiving updates through Funnel batch pipeline hydration.

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity whose data is indexed into OSv2 by Funnel batch pipelines, combining datasource data and user edits.

extractedEntities.name: Primary Key
extractedEntities.description: Property used by the Merge Changes stage to join changelog datasets and user edits, ensuring all changes for the same object are consolidated.

extractedEntities.name: Live Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Funnel pipeline variant that runs whenever datasources update and every six hours for user edit persistence, keeping object types current in production.

extractedEntities.name: Replacement Pipeline
extractedEntities.description: Funnel pipeline variant that performs full reindexing when schema changes require it, rebuilding the entire object database.

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology concept that generates user edits which are merged into Funnel pipelines, with edits applied immediately to indexes and persisted every six hours.

extractedEntities.name: Ontology Manager
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that displays hydration progress and manages Object Type configurations affected by Funnel pipelines.

keyInsights: Funnel batch pipelines are a four-stage internal process: Changelog, Merge Changes, Indexing, and Hydration, orchestrating data flow from datasources to OSv2.
User edits from Actions are merged with datasource changes by primary key, applied immediately to indexes but persisted to Foundry datasets every six hours.
Live pipelines run on datasource updates and every six hours for user edit persistence; replacement pipelines run for schema changes requiring full reindexing.
Hydration is the final step where OSv2 search nodes download index files to disk, making the Object Type queryable; progress is visible in Ontology Manager.
All intermediate datasets (changelog, merged, index) are owned and controlled by Funnel and not accessible to users.
The changelog stage uses incremental computation semantics with APPEND transactions to efficiently capture only data differences.
